relationship: §26.4's leapfrog integrator uses ∇U(θ) = −∇ log π(θ | y) at every half-step. Volume preservation (Proof 3) uses the Jacobian determinant.

Ergodic averages (§26.7) are integrals against the invariant measure π, estimated by Cesàro time-averaging. The Kac formula (renewal interpretation in Proof 4 Step 3) is a standard integration-by-parts-on-stopping-times argument.
Hamilton's equations (ẋ = ∂H/∂p, ṗ = −∂H/∂q) form an ODE system; §26.4 Def 4 and Rem 11 draw on ODE theory to contrast exact flow with the symplectic leapfrog approximation.

VI is the non-MCMC alternative for approximate posterior inference: pick a tractable q(θ), minimize KL(q ∥ p(· | y)). Trades exactness for speed. §26.10 Rem 26 names the contrast; formalml develops ELBO, mean-field, and normalizing flows.

SGLD (Welling–Teh 2011) and SGHMC (Chen–Fox–Guestrin 2014) replace the full-batch gradient in HMC / Langevin with a mini-batch gradient plus injected noise. Scales MCMC to large-n datasets at the cost of introducing asymptotic bias. §26.10 Rem 30.
SMC / particle filters are the alternative when the target is a sequence π_t indexed by time or annealing parameter. Parallel to MCMC's single-target framework. §26.10 Rem 31.
Stan, PyMC, NumPyro — domain-specific languages that compile user-specified models into NUTS samplers. Topic 26 motivates; formalml explores the compiler stack. §26.10 Rem 33.

The marginal likelihood $m(\mathbf{y}) = \int L(\theta)\pi(\theta)\,d\theta$ and posterior predictive $p(\tilde y \mid \mathbf{y}) = \int p(\tilde y \mid \theta)p(\theta \mid \mathbf{y})\,d\theta$ are integrals against the prior / posterior density. §25.4 Proof 1 does the conjugate-family closure calculation by identifying the integrand's kernel with a known density family.

§25.8 Proof 3 (BvM sketch) Taylor-expands the log-posterior in a neighborhood of $\hat\theta_{\text{MLE}}$; the leading Gaussian term is extracted via standard Taylor-with-remainder. The Laplace approximation remark is multivariate Taylor with a Hessian determinant.

§25.7 Rem on reparameterization invariance of Jeffreys prior uses the Jacobian change-of-variables formula to show $\pi_J(\phi) = \pi_J(\theta)|d\theta/d\phi|$ for any monotone reparameterization $\phi = g(\theta)$.

BNN posteriors are MVN near the MAP estimate by BvM (§25.8 Thm 5); the Laplace approximation (§25.8 Rem 16) is the canonical construction. formalml's BNN topic develops non-conjugate posterior approximation via variational inference, extending Topic 25's Laplace machinery to deep-network-scale models.

When conjugate priors break down (non-exponential-family likelihoods, hierarchical models beyond Topic 28) and MCMC is too slow, variational inference approximates the posterior with a tractable family $q(\theta)$ minimizing $\mathrm{KL}(q \| p(\cdot \mid \mathbf{y}))$. Topic 25 §25.10 pointers; formalml develops ELBO, mean-field VI, normalizing flows.

GPs are the infinite-dimensional generalization of Topic 25's finite-dimensional Bayesian framework: the prior is over functions rather than parameters, and the posterior is a conditional MVN (Topic 8 Thm 3 applied infinite-dimensionally).

Probabilistic programming languages (Stan, PyMC, NumPyro) automate the posterior computation Topic 25 does by hand — users specify prior × likelihood, the language handles sampling via HMC/NUTS. Topic 25's conjugate calculations show what the PPL would output for the easy cases.

§25.3 Thm 1 (posterior $\propto$ prior $\times$ likelihood) is the framework mixed-effects §5's PyMC fit operates inside; §25.5–§25.7's prior-selection theory — conjugate, weakly-informative, and Jeffreys — supplies the menu §5.1 chooses from on variance-component hyperparameters. §25.7's improper-prior theory contains the flat-improper case mixed-effects §4.2 marginalizes over the fixed effects to recover the REML profile likelihood.

The Laplace expansion of $m(\mathbf{y}) = \int L(\theta)\pi(\theta)\,d\theta$ uses multivariate Gaussian integration over a neighborhood of the posterior mode. §27.6 Proof 2 cites this mechanism directly.

Second-order Taylor expansion of $\log L(\theta)\pi(\theta)$ around $\hat\theta$ is the analytical backbone of the Occam–BIC equivalence (§27.6 Proof 2). The error term is $O_p(1/\sqrt{n})$ under Topic 14's regularity conditions.

Stacking is the M-open alternative to BMA: optimize predictive weights directly rather than derive them from marginal likelihoods. Critical when no candidate model is true. §27.10 Rem 26 sets up the contrast.

The evidence lower bound (ELBO) is a variational approximation to $\log m(\mathbf{y})$ derived from the Kullback–Leibler projection of the posterior onto a tractable family. Replaces MCMC+bridge for very large models at the cost of approximation bias.

RJMCMC (Green 1995) samples directly from the joint posterior over $(\mathcal{M}_k, \theta_k)$, providing BMA weights as a byproduct. An alternative to separate marginal-likelihood estimates when the model space is discrete and structured.

The Monte-Carlo error in §31.2 hinges on the $B \to \infty$ limit of the empirical average $B^{-1}\sum g(X^\ast_{(b)})$ to its conditional expectation — sequence-limit machinery from formalCalculus Track 1.

Lemma 1 in §31.3 (Polya's theorem) upgrades pointwise convergence of monotone functions with a continuous limit to uniform convergence. The partition argument is a classical uniform-convergence proof.
The Edgeworth expansion in §31.5 Theorem 5 is a Taylor-style series for a CDF around the standard-normal CDF, with coefficients driven by the cumulants of the statistic.

§31.4's bootstrap CIs are the foundation of conformal-prediction's non-residual precursor: construct a bootstrap distribution for the point prediction, then take percentile endpoints as the prediction-interval bounds.

§31.3's Theorem 3 underwrites bootstrapped model uncertainty: bag multiple bootstrap replicates of the training set, refit the model on each, and use the distribution of test-time predictions as a proxy for posterior uncertainty.
§31.9's bias correction is the standard debiasing technique for cross-validated risk estimates — CV risk underestimates test risk by an amount that the bootstrap-bias estimator quantifies.

§31.6's bootstrap hypothesis test is the distribution-free alternative to the parametric $t$-test for A/B significance — robust to non-Normal metric distributions that plague click-through and conversion data.
Pointwise limits of MGFs and characteristic functions, limsup in Berry–Esseen rate analysis.
Taylor series and power series expansions are the backbone of the MGF proof: log(1 + x) = x − x²/2 + O(x³) applied with x = O(1/n).
Taylor's theorem with remainder provides the rigorous bound on the remainder term in the MGF expansion. The delta method requires differentiability of the transformation g.

The mini-batch gradient is an average of B iid gradient samples. By the CLT, its fluctuations around the true gradient are Gaussian at scale σ/√B, which motivates the learning rate schedule η ∝ 1/√t and explains why larger batches reduce noise with diminishing returns.

Rademacher complexity bounds use symmetrization and CLT-type reasoning. The empirical Rademacher complexity converges to its expectation at rate 1/√n, giving the O(1/√n) generalization bound for bounded loss functions.

The Bernstein–von Mises theorem (the Bayesian CLT) proves that the posterior distribution √n(θ − θ₀) | X₁,…,Xₙ converges in total variation to N(0, I(θ₀)⁻¹), where I(θ₀) is the Fisher information. This is why Bayesian credible intervals and frequentist confidence intervals agree asymptotically.
The CLT gives the convergence rate of Monte Carlo estimators: the standard error of (1/n)Σf(Xᵢ) is σ_f/√n, leading to the canonical ±1.96σ/√n confidence band for Monte Carlo estimates.
The proof that independence of events implies independence of their complements uses basic algebraic manipulation of limits. The same epsilon-N framework from formalCalculus Topic 1.

Conditional independence is the structural assumption encoded in Bayesian networks. The factorization P(X₁,..., Xₙ) = ∏ P(Xᵢ | Parents(Xᵢ)) is the chain rule of probability combined with conditional independence assumptions read from the graph.

Conditional entropy H(X|Y) = -Σ P(x,y) log P(x|y) and mutual information I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X|Y) are defined via conditional probability. The chain rule for entropy H(X,Y) = H(X) + H(Y|X) mirrors the chain rule for probability.
The posterior distribution P(θ|data) ∝ P(data|θ)P(θ) is Bayes' theorem applied to parameter spaces. Every MCMC algorithm, every variational inference method targets this posterior.
Coverage probability P_θ(θ ∈ C(X)) is an integral of the sampling density over the event {θ ∈ C(X)}. The coverage diagnostic in §19.8 computes this integral numerically for binomial p.
Asymptotic coverage — the statement lim_{n→∞} P_θ(θ ∈ C_n(X)) = 1 − α — is a sequences-limits statement. The Wald-under-coverage-in-small-samples result in §19.5 is its finite-sample companion.

The profile likelihood ℓ_P(θ) = sup_ψ ℓ(θ, ψ) is a composition of an implicit function (ψ̂(θ) solves the nuisance score equation) and the joint likelihood. Differentiation under the sup is the Danskin envelope theorem — cited in §19.7 Rem 15.

Every deployed A/B-test platform reports a confidence interval on the treatment effect alongside the p-value. The Wilson interval is the industry default for rare-event conversion metrics (§19.5); bootstrap and always-valid confidence sequences are the two major extensions used in production.

GLM coefficient inference uses the Wald/LRT CI trio of §19.4 at the scale of every deployed logistic, Poisson, and gamma regression. The LRT-based deviance-difference CI is the numerical default in R's confint() on glm objects; Wald is the default in statsmodels.

PAC-Bayes generalization bounds are uniform (1−δ) confidence statements on the hypothesis class. The coverage-calibration theme of §19.8 generalizes to PAC-bound tightness diagnostics in the learning-theory literature.

Instrumental-variables regression, doubly-robust estimation, and difference-in-differences all report confidence intervals on the average treatment effect. The Wald CI is most common; sandwich variance estimators produce sandwich-Wald intervals. TOST (§19.9) is the equivalence-testing framework for noninferiority trials in biostatistics.
Computing E[X] for the Exponential and Gamma distributions requires integration by parts. The Gamma function recursion Γ(α+1) = αΓ(α) is proved by integration by parts.
The Normal MGF derivation uses 'completing the square' in the exponent. The Gamma function's properties connect to power series representations.
The Gaussian integral ∫e^{−z²/2}dz = √(2π) ensures the Normal PDF normalizes. The Gamma function satisfies Γ(n) = (n−1)! for positive integers, connecting continuous and discrete.
The Chi-squared distribution arises as Z² for Z~N(0,1), requiring the Jacobian. The Beta-Gamma connection Y₁/(Y₁+Y₂) ~ Beta(α,β) uses a bivariate change of variables.
The Gamma function Γ(α) = ∫₀^∞ t^{α-1}e^{-t}dt and all continuous PDFs on unbounded support are improper integrals.
Normal response → linear regression (identity link). Gamma response → Gamma GLM (log link) for positive-valued outcomes like insurance claims and time durations.
The Normal distribution is the marginal of every finite collection of GP outputs. The GP prior is an infinite-dimensional Gaussian.
Gaussian variational families are the default in VI. The Beta serves as the conjugate prior for Bernoulli likelihood in mean-field approximations.
The standard Normal N(0,I) is the base distribution. The change-of-variables formula transforms it through invertible mappings to model complex densities.
Beta-Bernoulli, Gamma-Poisson, and Normal-Normal are the three canonical conjugate pairs used in Bayesian inference and online learning.
The geometric series ∑r^k = 1/(1−r) and its derivatives are used in Geometric and Negative Binomial moment derivations. The limit (1−λ/n)^n → e^{−λ} drives the Poisson limit theorem.
The exponential power series e^x = ∑x^k/k! verifies that the Poisson PMF sums to 1 and drives MGF derivations for the Poisson distribution.

Logistic regression models Y|X ~ Bernoulli(σ(β^T X)). The natural parameter η = log(p/(1−p)) = β^T X is the log-odds, directly from the exponential family form. Cross-entropy loss is the negative Bernoulli log-likelihood.
Poisson regression (log link, count response) and Negative Binomial regression (overdispersed counts) use these distributions as the response family in GLMs.
Bernoulli Naive Bayes models binary feature vectors as independent Bernoulli random variables given the class label.
Pointwise vs uniform convergence of function sequences is the analytic backbone of every §32 theorem; §32.1 opens with that contrast.
§32.7's Hadamard differentiability is the uniform-convergence analog of pointwise differentiability; §32.7 Rem 17 cites continuity on function spaces directly.
Covering and bracketing numbers are metric-entropy constructions on $(\mathcal{F}, L^2(P))$; §32.6 uses the metric-entropy vocabulary introduced there.
§32.4's G-C-for-VC bound is the empirical-process core of PAC generalization bounds; formalML develops the learning-theory framing with Rademacher complexity and uniform convergence of risk.
PAC-Bayes lifts Topic 32's function-class complexity arguments to a posterior-over-hypotheses setting; McAllester's 1999 bound uses the same symmetrization technique §32.4 introduces.
Conformal prediction constructs distribution-free CIs via exchangeability rather than empirical-process asymptotics; the two meet at Topic 29 §29.5's DKW inequality and Topic 32's uniform bands.
M-estimator asymptotics — the modern nonparametric-plus-parametric-component framework — is built on local empirical-process theory (local Donsker, local bracketing), which §32.10 Rem 22 defers.

Targeted maximum likelihood, augmented inverse-propensity weighting, and efficient-influence-function methods all rest on the functional delta method (§32.7) applied to nuisance-parameter empirical processes.

Fisher–Tippett–Gnedenko asymptotics for the maximum $X_{(n)}$ and the generalized Pareto; forward-pointed from Topic 29 §29.10 Rem 18, orthogonal to §32 but listed here for completeness of the formalML handoff.
Computing E[X] for the Exponential and Gamma distributions requires integration by parts. The formula ∫x·λe^{−λx}dx = 1/λ is a standard application.
The absolute convergence condition E[|X|] < ∞ ensures the expectation sum/integral is well-defined. Interchange of limit and expectation (dominated convergence) is used in MGF proofs.
The MGF expansion M_X(t) = Σ E[X^n] t^n/n! is a Taylor series. The proof that M^{(n)}(0) = E[X^n] uses term-by-term differentiation of the power series.
The bias-variance decomposition E[(Y−f̂)²] = Bias² + Variance + Noise is a direct application of Eve's law and the variance decomposition proved here.
Cross-entropy H(p,q) = −E_p[log q] and KL divergence D_KL(p||q) = E_p[log(p/q)] are expectations. Jensen's inequality immediately gives D_KL ≥ 0.

The training objective Risk(f) = E[ℓ(Y, f(X))] is an expectation over the data distribution. Stochastic gradient descent estimates ∇E[L] with ∇L on minibatches — linearity of expectation justifies unbiased gradient estimates.
The log-partition function's moment-generating properties use Taylor expansion arguments. The relationship between A(η) and the moment-generating function connects to power series.
The Fisher information I(η) = A''(η) defines a Riemannian metric on the parameter space. Natural gradient descent uses this metric for parameter-invariant optimization.
Every GLM requires an exponential family response distribution. The canonical link function is (A')⁻¹. Logistic, Poisson, and Gamma regression are instances.
Exponential family variational distributions enable natural gradient VI. The ELBO has a closed-form expectation when both the prior and variational distribution are in the same exponential family.

The score $\nabla_{\boldsymbol\beta}\ell$ and Hessian $\nabla^2_{\boldsymbol\beta}\ell$ are vector- and matrix-valued partial derivatives over $\boldsymbol\beta \in \mathbb{R}^{p+1}$. §22.3 Proofs 1 and 2 chain the gradient through the link composition $\boldsymbol\mu = g^{-1}(\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol\beta)$ — the chain rule and Jacobian computations are the calculus content.

§22.3 Thm 1's Hessian $\mathcal{H}(\boldsymbol\beta) = -\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{W}\mathbf{X}$ uses the standard quadratic-form derivative $\partial(\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x})/\partial\mathbf{x} = (\mathbf{A} + \mathbf{A}^\top)\mathbf{x}$ and the chain rule for vector-by-vector derivatives. The IRLS WLS-update step in §22.3 Proof 2 is a matrix-calculus identity recognizable on sight to anyone who has seen the OLS normal-equations derivation in matrix form.

§22.3 Thm 3 (asymptotic normality of $\hat{\boldsymbol\beta}$) and §22.7 Thm 6 (deviance LRT $\to \chi^2_k$) both rest on multivariate Taylor expansions of the log-likelihood around the MLE. The $\sqrt{n}$ scaling in the asymptotic distribution is exactly the Taylor-series remainder bound.

§22.4 Rem 9 forward-points: cross-entropy loss in deep learning is literally the negative Bernoulli log-likelihood per observation, and the final-layer sigmoid is a logistic GLM. Every binary classifier built on top of a neural network is fitting a logistic GLM in the last layer — the only difference is that the linear predictor is replaced by a learned representation.

§22.10 Rem 26 forward-points: the ML-scale treatment covers multiclass softmax (multinomial GLM), the unifying information-geometry framing (natural-gradient = Fisher scoring = IRLS in continuous time), and the deep-learning final-layer GLM perspective. Topic 22 is the math; the formalml topic is the engineering.

§22.10 Rem 25 forward-points: GLMMs add random effects $\mathbf{b} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \boldsymbol\Sigma)$ to the linear predictor for clustered or hierarchical data. Topic 22 covers the fixed-effects-only GLM; the random-effects machinery requires Track 7's Bayesian foundations and is treated at scale on formalml.
Inverse-probability-weighted estimators, doubly-robust estimators, and TMLE all use GLMs as the propensity / outcome model components. §22.10 Rem 27 catalog-points; the methodology lives at formalml.

When $p \gg n$ the GLM design matrix is rank-deficient and ordinary IRLS fails. §22.10 Rem 28 forward-points to the high-dimensional treatment: penalized GLMs ([Topic 23](/topics/regularization-and-penalized-estimation) is the Track-6 entry) plus the minimax theory and double-descent phenomenon at formalml.

The Jacobian determinant in §28.9 Thm 7's non-centered volume-preservation proof uses multivariate change of variables on the block-triangular map $(\tilde\theta, \mu, \tau) \mapsto (\theta, \mu, \tau)$.
Stein's identity in §28.5 is integration by parts applied coordinatewise to $g(X) = X/\|X\|^2$; the full proof relies on the multivariate gradient of this gradient field.
The horseshoe, regularized horseshoe, spike-and-slab, and R2-D2 priors are the continuous-shrinkage alternatives to the hierarchical-Normal prior that §28.6 develops. §28.10 Rem 23 pointer.
Mean-field and structured VI for hierarchical models at scale; stochastic VI (Hoffman et al. 2013) and black-box VI (Ranganath et al. 2014). §28.10 Rem 24 pointer.
Hierarchical priors over weights, MC-dropout, deep ensembles, SG-MCMC over NN posteriors. §28.10 Rem 24 pointer.
Dirichlet process, CRP, hierarchical DP, stick-breaking. §28.10 Rem 25 pointer.
MAML and hierarchical Bayes over task distributions; the machine-learning reading of §28's group-level hyperparameters. §28.10 Ex 14 pointer.
REML, lme4, Stan-style GLMMs, recommender-system random-effects structures. §28.8 Rem 20 pointer.

Rejection-region probabilities under both $H_0$ and $H_\theta$ are integrals of the sampling density over $R$. Power $\beta(\theta) = \int_R f(x; \theta)\,dx$ is a calculus-of-variations object in $\theta$.

Convergence in distribution is the language of asymptotic null distributions (§17.9). Consistency of tests — power $\to 1$ as $n \to \infty$ under any fixed alternative — is a sequences-limits statement.
The power function $\beta(\theta)$ is differentiated to characterize most-powerful tests (previewed in §17.4, full treatment in Topic 18). The score test uses $\partial_\theta \ell(\theta)$ directly.

A/B testing is hypothesis testing applied at platform scale. The null 'Design A and Design B have the same conversion rate' is a two-sample proportion test; sample-size calculations are §17.4 power calculations with Type II error controlled. Sequential testing, always-valid confidence intervals, and CUPED variance reduction are modern extensions of this core framework.

PAC generalization bounds are uniform-confidence statements of the form $P(\sup_h |\hat R(h) - R(h)| > \epsilon) \le \delta$ — Type-I-error-style guarantees. VC bounds and Rademacher complexity provide the concentration inequalities that play the role of null distributions in statistical learning.

Cross-validation, AIC, BIC, and held-out likelihood are ML-native alternatives to nested hypothesis testing. §17.11 Remark 14 frames the conceptual contrast: classical testing asks 'reject $H_0$?'; ML model comparison asks 'which model predicts better?' — different questions, different machinery.

Randomized experiments use t-tests and z-tests for average treatment effects; falsification and placebo tests are hypothesis tests for identification assumptions. The score test reappears as a specification test in instrumental-variables regression.

§30.4's bias derivation Taylor-expands $f$ around $x$ to second order; §30.6's optimization Taylor-expands AMISE around $h^\ast$. Both reduce to first- and second-order conditions on standard Taylor expansions.
The MISE integral $\int \int (\hat f_h(x) - f(x))^2 f(y)\, dy\, dx$ requires comfort with interchanging expectation and integration — Fubini–Tonelli from multivariable integration.
§30.6 Thm 5 (Epanechnikov optimality) uses Lagrangian calculus-of-variations — the Euler–Lagrange equation on a quadratic functional with linear constraints.
Nadaraya–Watson kernel regression is the covariate-conditional extension of KDE: average $Y_i$ values weighted by $K_h(x - X_i)$. §30.9 Rem 17 pointer; full development forthcoming on formalml.

Fan–Gijbels 1996 local polynomial regression corrects KDE's boundary bias and generalizes to the regression setting. §30.5 Rem 9 name-checks the local-linear-at-boundary estimator; forthcoming on formalml.

Rezende–Mohamed 2015 normalizing flows replace the kernel-average-of-bumps with a learned invertible transformation of a simple base density. §30.10 Rem 20 pointer; orthogonal to classical AMISE theory.
Mean-shift clustering uses gradient ascent on $\hat f_h$ to find local modes — the KDE's stationary points *are* the cluster centers. §30.9 Rem 18 pointer; forthcoming on formalml.
Sugiyama–Suzuki–Kanamori 2012 two-sample density-ratio estimation, the foundation of covariate-shift correction. §30.10 Rem 22 pointer; forthcoming on formalml.
Limits for the n → ∞ asymptotics in the LDP statement and the Cramér rate function evaluation.

First-order optimization of the Chernoff bound over t (the tilting parameter). The Legendre transform I(x) = sup_t(tx − log M(t)) is a convex conjugate, requiring differentiation to evaluate. Taylor's theorem enters the Hoeffding lemma.
Taylor expansion of e^x and log(1 + x) in the sub-Gaussian characterization and the Hoeffding lemma. The MGF expansion M(t) = Σ E[X^n] t^n / n! from Topic 4.
Expectation integrals in MGF computations. The Cramér rate function involves a supremum over an integral expression.

PAC learning bounds use Hoeffding: P(|R(ĥ) − R̂ₙ(ĥ)| > ε) ≤ 2|H| exp(−2nε²). Rademacher symmetrization uses McDiarmid's inequality. The O(1/√n) generalization rate for bounded loss classes originates from these concentration bounds.

The Johnson–Lindenstrauss lemma proves that random projections from ℝᵈ to ℝᵏ with k = O(ε⁻² log n) approximately preserve pairwise distances among n points. The proof uses sub-Gaussian concentration of the projected inner products.
The Gaussian mechanism adds N(0, σ²) noise with σ calibrated via sub-Gaussian tail bounds: σ = Δ√(2 ln(1.25/δ))/ε. Concentrated differential privacy (zCDP) uses the sub-Gaussian MGF bound directly.
Sub-Gaussian gradient noise analysis justifies step-size schedules. Azuma–Hoeffding applies to the martingale formed by cumulative SGD gradient errors, giving non-asymptotic convergence rates.

Hoeffding gives non-asymptotic confidence bands for bounded Monte Carlo estimators: n ≥ (b−a)² ln(2/δ)/(2ε²) samples suffice for ε-accuracy with probability 1 − δ. This complements the CLT's asymptotic ±1.96σ/√n band.

The SLLN is an almost sure convergence statement — P(lim X̄ₙ = μ) = 1 — which requires the formal definition of limits and the ε-N proof technique. Limsup and liminf appear in the Borel–Cantelli characterization used in the proof.

The Borel–Cantelli lemma — used in both the truncation step of Etemadi's proof and the Glivenko–Cantelli theorem — requires that Σ P(Aₙ) < ∞, a series convergence condition. The variance series Σ Var(Yᵢ)/i² < ∞ requires comparison tests.
The dominated convergence theorem is the key tool in the truncation argument: E[X₁ 1(|X₁| ≤ n)] → E[X₁] as n → ∞, and E[X₁² 1(|X₁| ≤ n)]/n → 0. Both limits are justified by DCT.

SGD convergence under Robbins–Monro conditions (Σ αₜ = ∞, Σ αₜ² < ∞) is proved via the SLLN and martingale convergence. Polyak averaging — computing the running mean of SGD iterates — converges a.s. to the optimum by the SLLN.

PAC learning bounds are WLLN statements: P(|R̂(h) − R(h)| > ε) ≤ δ. Uniform convergence over hypothesis classes (Vapnik–Chervonenkis theory) extends the Glivenko–Cantelli theorem from indicators to function classes.

The SLLN is the theoretical foundation of all Monte Carlo methods. [Topic 26 §26.7 Theorem 5](/topics/bayesian-computation-and-mcmc#section-26-7) proves the Markov-chain ergodic theorem — the generalization of Theorem 10.5 (Kolmogorov/Etemadi SLLN) to dependent sequences; the additional ingredients beyond iid are irreducibility, aperiodicity, and positive recurrence. Importance sampling convergence is the SLLN applied under a change of measure.

ERM consistency — the minimizer of the empirical risk converges to the minimizer of the population risk — requires uniform convergence of R̂ₙ(θ) → R(θ) over θ, which is a generalization of the Glivenko–Cantelli theorem.

The Wilks proof's Step 2 is Taylor's theorem with remainder, applied to the log-likelihood $\ell(\theta)$ around the MLE $\hat\theta_n$. Step 3 uses the first-order condition $\ell'(\hat\theta_n) = 0$ — an interior-maximum calculus fact.

The Neyman-Pearson proof integrates the indicator-function inequality $(\varphi^* - \varphi)(f_1 - k f_0) \ge 0$ over the sample space to convert a pointwise statement into an expectation inequality. The non-central $\chi^2$ series in §18.9 is a sum of integrals against shifted-df Gamma densities.

Convergence in distribution (the Wilks conclusion $-2\log\Lambda_n \xrightarrow{d} \chi^2_1$) and in probability ($\hat\theta_n \xrightarrow{P} \theta_0$) are both rigorous limit statements. The $O_P / o_P$ algebra used in Step 4 of the Wilks proof is formal bookkeeping for these limits.

A/B platforms default to Wald z-tests, but as §18.8 shows, for boundary-rare events (conversion rates near 0 or 1) the Wald test's reparameterization dependence can flip the rejection decision. Modern platforms (Statsig, GrowthBook) increasingly offer LRT or log-odds-ratio testing for this regime — direct applications of the Thm 6 invariance argument.

Wald/Score/LRT tests for GLM coefficients are the direct vector-θ generalization of §18.7. The score test's 'only fit the null model' advantage powers stepwise forward selection; the LRT-via-deviance-difference is the workhorse of nested-model comparison in logistic and Poisson regression.

PAC-Bayes and VC-dimension generalization bounds are uniform-concentration statements whose proofs mirror the indicator-function comparison of Proof 1 (Neyman-Pearson). The 'MP test = LR test' duality reappears as the Bayes classifier in decision-theoretic learning: under 0-1 loss the likelihood ratio $f_1 / f_0$ is the optimal score.

Wilks' theorem is the frequentist backbone of the likelihood-ratio test for nested models — the answer to 'did the extra parameters help?' The Bayesian analog is the Bayes factor ([Topic 27 §27.4](/topics/bayesian-model-comparison-and-bma#section-27-4)); the ML-native analog is cross-validated held-out likelihood.

Theorem 1 (OLS-as-orthogonal-projection) is an application of the projection theorem in finite-dimensional inner-product spaces: the unique closest point in $\text{col}(\mathbf{X})$ to $\mathbf{y}$ is the orthogonal projection $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$, and the residual $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{y} - \hat{\mathbf{y}}$ is orthogonal to every vector in $\text{col}(\mathbf{X})$. The Pythagorean decomposition step in Proof 1 uses the same identity $\|\mathbf{a} + \mathbf{b}\|^2 = \|\mathbf{a}\|^2 + \|\mathbf{b}\|^2$ when $\mathbf{a} \perp \mathbf{b}$.

§21.7 characterizes the hat matrix $\mathbf{H}$ as an orthogonal projector — idempotent, symmetric, with $\text{rank}(\mathbf{H}) = \text{tr}(\mathbf{H}) = p + 1$. The spectral decomposition of a symmetric idempotent matrix (eigenvalues $\in \{0, 1\}$) is the calculus-side fact the proof invokes. §21.8 Proof 9 uses Cochran's theorem on quadratic forms in Normal vectors to split $\text{SSE}_0 - \text{SSE}_1$ and $\text{SSE}_1$ into independent $\chi^2$ components.

§21.4's matrix-form pivot rests on linear algebra essentials: $\mathbf{X}$ is $n \times (p+1)$, $\text{col}(\mathbf{X}) \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ is a $(p+1)$-dimensional subspace under full rank, and $\hat{\mathbf{y}} \in \text{col}(\mathbf{X})$ is the projection. No machinery beyond undergraduate linear algebra is assumed.

Topic 22 is the immediate successor — logistic, Poisson, and gamma regression drop the Normal-errors assumption by composing a linear predictor with a link function and fitting via IRLS. The Wald / Score / LRT trio of §18.7 reappears as the three standard GLM inference procedures (deviance, z-tests, score). §21.10 Rem 22 states the bridge.

Topic 21's scope boundary in §21.1 Rem 1 (linear in parameters, not in predictors) licenses polynomial basis expansions, interaction terms, and spline features — all of which preserve linearity in $\boldsymbol\beta$ and thus preserve Gauss–Markov and the F-test. The forward pointer to Track 8's kernel regression (§21.10 Rem 26) handles the nonparametric case.

Instrumental variables, difference-in-differences, regression discontinuity — all live in the linear-model framework where OLS + identifying restrictions give unbiased causal estimates. §21.10 Rem 27 points here; the prerequisite is §21.4's matrix form and §21.8's inference machinery.

When $p > n$, $\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X}$ is singular and OLS fails. [Topic 23](/topics/regularization-and-penalized-estimation) (ridge, lasso) is the Track-6 answer; the high-dimensional theory (double descent, minimax rates) lives at formalml. §21.10 Rem 28 states the forward pointer.

§21.6 Gauss–Markov (OLS as BLUE under homoscedastic errors) is the result mixed-effects §2.4's GLS-as-BLUE / Aitken whitening specializes: the whitening map $\boldsymbol\Omega^{-1/2}$ reduces GLS on the correlated-error system to OLS on a whitened system, and BLUE-preservation under whitening follows directly from applying §21.6 to the transformed design. Mixed-effects §2's GLS proof is therefore §21.6 plus a change of basis.

The score function is ∂/∂θ log L(θ), and the asymptotic-normality proof Taylor-expands the score around θ₀. Newton-Raphson requires the second derivative ∂²/∂θ² log L(θ) = −observed Fisher information.
Regularity conditions for the MLE require interchange of derivative and integral — the same conditions as the CRLB in Topic 13.
Consistency is convergence in probability; the asymptotic-normality proof uses Slutsky's theorem and convergence of the observed information to the expected information.

MLE for a categorical model minimizes cross-entropy loss. The log-likelihood ℓ(θ) = Σ log f(xᵢ; θ) is (up to sign and an empirical-distribution constant) the negative cross-entropy between the empirical distribution and the model.
Logistic regression is MLE for a Bernoulli GLM with logit link. The log-likelihood has no closed-form MLE; Newton-Raphson (IRLS) computes it iteratively.
MAP estimation adds a prior log-density to the log-likelihood: log L(θ) + log π(θ). Gaussian prior → L² penalty; Laplace prior → L¹ penalty. Regularized MLE is the Bayesian bridge.
EM iteratively maximizes a lower bound on the log-likelihood for latent-variable models (mixtures, HMMs). Each M-step is an MLE of the complete-data log-likelihood.

Topic 14's general MLE small-sample bias theory — concretely the $(n-p)/n$ downward bias of the Normal-model variance MLE relative to the unbiased $S^2$ that divides by $n-p$ — is the prototype mixed-effects §4.1 invokes for variance components. The §4.2 REML correction generalizes the same degrees-of-freedom adjustment from a single residual $\sigma^2$ to the full random-effects covariance $\boldsymbol\Sigma$, recovering Topic 14's correction as the fixed-design special case.
The delta method uses the Jacobian of the moment-to-parameter map; the M-estimator sandwich formula uses partial derivatives of ψ(x; θ) with respect to θ.
The sandwich variance A⁻¹BA⁻¹ᵀ is a matrix product; positive-definiteness of the covariance of sample moments is a quadratic-form argument.
MoM consistency is convergence in probability inherited from the LLN; Slutsky's theorem appears in the asymptotic normality derivation.

M-estimation IS empirical risk minimization: θ̂ minimizes the empirical average of a loss ρ(X; θ), equivalently solves Σ ψ(X; θ) = 0 where ψ = ∇_θ ρ. Every ERM estimator with a smooth convex loss is an M-estimator.

Huber and Tukey M-estimators are the foundation of modern robust regression. The breakdown point and influence function quantify resistance to outliers. Huber loss is used routinely in ML for regression under heavy-tailed noise.

Quantile regression minimizes the check loss ρ_τ(u) = u(τ − 𝟙(u < 0)); its ψ is non-smooth but falls within the general M-estimation framework (modulo regularity). The scalar-intercept case is Topic 29 §29.4/§29.6; quantile regression is the covariate-conditional extension.

GMM (Hansen 1982) generalizes MoM to over-identified systems — more moment equations than parameters — solved by weighted least squares on the moment residuals. Previewed in §15.12 as the forward link.

Akaike's proof (§24.3 Proof 1) Taylor-expands $\ell(\theta)$ around $\theta_0$ in $p+1$ dimensions; the BIC-Laplace proof (§24.4 Proof 2) does multivariate Laplace with a Hessian determinant. Both rely on the second-order Taylor theorem with remainder.

§24.5 Stone's hat-matrix identity uses the trace cyclicity property; §24.8 derives ridge effective DOF $\sum_j d_j^2/(d_j^2+\lambda)$ in SVD coordinates. Both rely on standard matrix-calculus identities from the formalCalculus track.

After AIC/BIC/CV selection, naive Wald CIs are invalid because the selection event is data-dependent. Debiased lasso (Zhang–Zhang 2014), selective conditioning (Lee–Sun–Sun–Taylor 2016), and cross-fitting (Chernozhukov et al. 2018) restore validity. §24.10 Rem 24 forward-points.
BIC approximates the log marginal likelihood of a single model. BMA weights predictions by posterior model probabilities. §24.4 Rem 7 flags; §24.10 Rem 23 forward-points; full treatment Track 7.
Chernozhukov et al. 2018's double/debiased ML formalism uses cross-fitting for valid causal inference after ML-selected nuisance models. §24.10 Rem 24.

When $p \gg n$, likelihood-based IC degenerates. Extended BIC (Chen–Chen 2008), stability selection (Meinshausen–Bühlmann 2010), and knockoffs (Barber–Candès 2015) extend the framework. §24.10 Rem 25.
Vapnik's SRM generalizes information criteria to function classes via VC dimension or Rademacher complexity. §24.10 Rem 26.

The formal definitions of almost sure convergence and convergence in probability are limit statements. The ε-N definition of limits (for sequences) is the template. Limsup and liminf appear in the Borel–Cantelli characterization of a.s. convergence.

The Borel–Cantelli lemma — the standard tool for proving a.s. convergence — requires that ΣP(An) < ∞, a series convergence condition. The first Borel–Cantelli is used in the proof that convergence in probability implies a.s. convergence along a subsequence.

Lp convergence is defined via the integral norm E[|Xn − X|^p] = ∫|Xn − X|^p dP. The dominated convergence theorem is used to interchange limits and integrals when proving that Lp implies convergence in probability.

SGD convergence analysis is an exercise in modes of convergence. With decreasing step sizes satisfying the Robbins–Monro conditions, the iterates converge almost surely to a local minimum. With constant step size, convergence is only in probability to a neighborhood.

PAC learning bounds state that the empirical risk converges to the true risk in probability: P(|R̂(h) − R(h)| > ε) ≤ δ. The uniform convergence version (over hypothesis classes) uses the Glivenko–Cantelli theorem — a.s. convergence of the empirical CDF.

The Bernstein–von Mises theorem states that the posterior distribution converges in total variation (hence in distribution) to a Normal centered at the MLE. This is the frequentist justification for Bayesian inference.

Value iteration and Q-learning produce sequences of value functions that converge almost surely to the optimal value function under appropriate step-size schedules — the same Robbins–Monro machinery as SGD.
Proof 1 (Bonferroni) is the union bound applied once. The union bound on the event $\{V \ge 1\} = \bigcup_{i \in H_0} \{p_i \le \alpha/m\}$ is the only analytical tool Bonferroni requires.

Proof 3 (Holm) is an induction on the number of rejections $r$. The base case $r = 1$ reduces to Bonferroni; the inductive step uses the threshold $\alpha/(m - r + 1)$ on the remaining $m - r + 1$ hypotheses.

Every high-dimensional feature-selection procedure inherits a multiple-testing problem. BH is the default tool for selecting significant coefficients in deployed scikit-learn and R glmnet pipelines; §20.7 gives the theory, and §20.8 Thm 6 handles the positive-dependence regime regression inevitably lives in.

The Barber–Candès (2015) knockoffs framework is a direct descendent of BH: it constructs synthetic 'knockoff' features, computes test statistics per feature, and applies a BH-like step-up. The $\alpha \cdot \pi_0$ control of §20.7 Thm 5 is the result it generalizes. §20.10 Rem 28 states the forward pointer.

The single-test framework of §20.3 assumes the $m$ hypotheses and the stopping rule are fixed in advance. Always-valid confidence sequences (Howard et al. 2021) and e-processes (Vovk 2019, Grünwald et al. 2024) dispense with this by using supermartingale / anytime bounds. §20.10 Rem 26 states the forward pointer.

The streaming generalization of BH — Javanmard–Montanari 2018 Online-BH and Ramdas et al. 2018 SAFFRON / LORD — allows hypotheses to arrive sequentially with FDR control at each time. §20.10 Rem 27 points forward to the full treatment.
Marginalizing a p-dimensional joint density requires iterated integrals over R^{p-q}. The general framework in §2 assumes Fubini's theorem for exchanging the order of integration.
The multivariate change of variables formula (§8) uses the Jacobian determinant. The whitening transform and Cholesky decomposition are special cases of affine changes of variables.
The spectral decomposition Σ = QΛQ^T gives the principal axes of the MVN distribution. Eigenvalues are the variances along principal axes. This is the mathematical core of PCA.

A GP defines a joint MVN over function values at any finite set of inputs. The conditional MVN formula from §4 IS the GP prediction formula — conditional mean = posterior mean, conditional covariance = posterior uncertainty.

PCA is the eigendecomposition of the sample covariance matrix. The eigenvectors are the principal directions, and the eigenvalues are the explained variances. The MVN geometry from §7 makes this concrete.

The default variational family is the multivariate Normal — full-covariance or diagonal (mean-field). The reparameterization trick for VAEs uses the Cholesky sampling formula from §8: z = μ + Lε where ε ~ N(0,I).
Normalizing flows learn an invertible transform g from N(0,I) to a complex target. The change of variables formula f_Y(y) = f_Z(g^{-1}(y)) |det J| from §8 is the core mathematical tool.
Weight priors w ~ N_p(0, σ²I) are multivariate Normal. The posterior p(w|D) is approximately MVN for well-specified models. The Laplace approximation uses the Hessian as the inverse covariance.

§4 Thm 3's conditional-MVN formula $\boldsymbol\mu_{1|2} = \boldsymbol\mu_1 + \boldsymbol\Sigma_{12}\boldsymbol\Sigma_{22}^{-1}(\mathbf{x}_2 - \boldsymbol\mu_2)$ (the Schur-complement reading) is the pure-probability identity mixed-effects §3 specializes to derive BLUP as the posterior mean $\mathrm{Cov}(\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{y})\,\mathrm{Cov}(\mathbf{y})^{-1}(\mathbf{y} - \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{y}])$. Mixed-effects §2's marginal-distribution theorem reduces to the §3 affine-transform result here — linear maps preserve MVN.
The joint-density derivation in §29.2 is a Jacobian change-of-variables calculation over the ordered simplex. Rényi's proof (§29.3) uses the same machinery on the spacings map.
Bahadur's proof (§29.6) Taylor-expands $F$ around $\xi_p$ and controls the remainder — the first-order term is load-bearing, the second-order term is $O(1/n)$.

Fisher–Tippett–Gnedenko trichotomy for the limit of properly normalized $X_{(n)}$. Topic 29 covers $\hat\xi_p$ at interior $p$; EVT covers $\hat\xi_p$ as $p \to 1$. §29.10 Rem 18 pointer; forthcoming on formalml.

Koenker–Bassett check-loss regression. Topic 29 handles the scalar-intercept case via §29.4/§29.6; quantile regression is the covariate-conditional extension. §29.10 Rem 19 pointer; forthcoming on formalml.
Wilcoxon signed-rank, Mann–Whitney U, Kruskal–Wallis. Permutation-distribution machinery is orthogonal to Bahadur asymptotics and deferred. §29.10 Rem 20 pointer; forthcoming on formalml.
Vovk–Gammerman–Shafer distribution-free prediction. §29.10 Rem 21 motivates via §29.7's quantile-CI perspective; forthcoming on formalml.
Tukey / Mahalanobis / halfspace depths — multivariate generalizations of the quantile function. §29.10 Rem 22 pointer; forthcoming on formalml.
Consistency is a convergence statement — θ̂ₙ →ᵖ θ or θ̂ₙ →ᵃ·ˢ· θ — which requires the formal ε-δ definition of limits and the convergence modes defined in Topic 9.

The score function is the derivative ∂/∂θ log f(X; θ), and Fisher information is E[(∂/∂θ log f)²]. The Cramér-Rao proof requires interchange of differentiation and integration under regularity conditions.
The regularity conditions for the Cramér-Rao bound require ∂/∂θ ∫ f(x;θ) dx = ∫ (∂/∂θ) f(x;θ) dx — interchange of derivative and integral, justified by dominated convergence.
The MSE decomposition Bias² + Variance is the formal version of the ML bias-variance tradeoff. Regularization, model selection, and ensemble methods all navigate this tradeoff.
L2 regularization (ridge regression) deliberately introduces bias to reduce variance — a James-Stein-type shrinkage. The optimal regularization parameter minimizes MSE, not bias alone.

The mini-batch gradient is a biased estimator of the full gradient (if the batch is a strict subset). Its variance is σ²/B, where B is the batch size. The learning rate schedule navigates the bias-variance tradeoff in optimization.

Bagging reduces variance (averaging independent estimators) without increasing bias. Boosting reduces bias by sequentially fitting residuals. Both strategies are applications of the MSE decomposition.

CDF properties (Theorem 1) use limits at infinity and continuity of probability from Topic 1. The proof that F(x) → 0 as x → -∞ and F(x) → 1 as x → ∞ requires the convergence framework from formalCalculus.
The change of variables formula for PDFs (Theorem 7) uses the Jacobian and the substitution rule for integrals. The multidimensional version uses the Jacobian determinant.

Normalizing flows learn invertible transformations of simple distributions. The change of variables formula f_Y(y) = f_X(g⁻¹(y)) · |det J_{g⁻¹}(y)| is the mathematical backbone — each flow layer applies this formula.
Fisher information is defined via the score function ∂/∂θ log f(x; θ), which requires the PDF parameterization. The statistical manifold is the space of distributions.
Variational inference approximates posterior distributions by minimizing KL divergence between density functions. The conditional PDF framework enables the ELBO decomposition.

§23.3 Thm 2 (lasso KKT) and §23.4 Thm 6 (coord-descent convergence) both rely on subgradient calculus and the optimality conditions for convex non-smooth functions. The subgradient of $|\beta_j|$ — single-valued $\text{sign}(\beta_j)$ for $\beta_j \neq 0$ and the interval $[-1, 1]$ at $\beta_j = 0$ — is the load-bearing convex-analysis fact. Tseng 2001's coordinate-descent convergence theorem requires the separability hypothesis $f = g + \sum_j h_j$ with $g$ smooth and each $h_j$ convex.

Ridge's closed-form $(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X} + \lambda\mathbf{I})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y}$ uses the standard quadratic-form derivative $\partial(\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x})/\partial\mathbf{x} = (\mathbf{A} + \mathbf{A}^\top)\mathbf{x}$ from Topic 21. The §23.6 penalized-IRLS update reuses Topic 22's Hessian-of-log-likelihood machinery.

MSE decomposition (§23.3 Proof 1) and the SVD-coordinate rotation $\boldsymbol\gamma = \mathbf{V}^\top\boldsymbol\beta$ require multivariate Taylor expansion and the rotational invariance of $\|\cdot\|_2$. The ridge MSE-improvement proof's first-order Taylor at $\lambda = 0$ uses the standard $f(\lambda) = f(0) + f'(0)\lambda + O(\lambda^2)$ argument.

§23.10 Rem 28 forward-points to the ML-scale treatment of regularization including weight decay, dropout (as implicit regularization), data augmentation, label smoothing, and early stopping. The penalty-as-prior framing of §23.7 Thm 5 generalizes: every regularizer in deep learning is a prior in disguise.

§23.7 Rem 18 forward-points: weight decay in stochastic gradient descent and AdamW (decoupled weight decay) are MAP estimation with a Gaussian prior, exactly as Thm 5 says. The implementation difference between L² regularization and decoupled weight decay matters for Adam-family optimizers but not for vanilla SGD.

§23.10 Rem 29 forward-points to the asymptotic theory under $p \gg n$: oracle inequalities, restricted-eigenvalue conditions (Bickel–Ritov–Tsybakov 2009), the lasso $\ell_2$-consistency rate (Wainwright 2019 Thm 7.13), debiased-lasso post-selection inference (Javanmard–Montanari 2014), and minimax bounds. Topic 23 stops at lasso $\ell_2$-consistency stated-only (§23.10 Thm 7); the proofs and tightness arguments live at formalml.

Modern overparameterized regimes (neural networks, random-features models) violate Topic 23's $p < n$ assumption. The double-descent phenomenon — risk curve as a function of model complexity has a second descent past the interpolation threshold — is treated at formalml. Implicit regularization (SGD's bias toward minimum-norm solutions) plays the role of explicit ridge in those regimes.
Continuity of probability (Theorem 6) uses convergence of partial sums. The proof relies on the same telescoping-sum argument and epsilon-N convergence framework developed in formalCalculus.
The sigma-algebra and probability measure defined here generalize to abstract measure spaces. The Kolmogorov axioms are the probability specialization of the Lebesgue measure axioms.
Entropy H(P) = -sum p_i log p_i is defined on probability distributions over finite sample spaces. The axioms ensure that probability distributions — and therefore entropy — are well-defined.

The factorization theorem and Rao-Blackwell proofs use joint-density integration and the interchange of integration with conditional expectation (Fubini). The PKD proof integrates a separable functional equation in both arguments.
The PKD proof cross-differentiates the log-factorization in the data argument and the parameter, deriving the separable equation that characterizes the exponential-family form.
Almost-sure equality in the Rao-Blackwell and Basu conclusions; asymptotic-sufficiency remarks in §16.12 use convergence in probability.

The information bottleneck method finds a compressed representation $T$ of the data $X$ that preserves information about a target $Y$. In the special case $\theta = Y$ and $T$ is the sufficient statistic, the bottleneck recovers the Topic 16 notion of sufficiency. Learned sufficient statistics are the non-parametric analog.

Self-supervised learning objectives (contrastive, masked-modeling) implicitly discover sufficient statistics of $X$ for downstream tasks. The Rao-Blackwellization intuition — that optimal estimators are functions of $T$ — reappears as the rationale for pretrained-feature reuse.

Variational bounds on the mutual information between $T$ and $\theta$ give tractable proxies for sufficiency in deep models. The exp-family structure from Topic 7 §7.7 gives the closed-form ELBO updates.

MDL codes data as parameter + residual; under exp-family sufficiency, the optimal parameter code uses exactly the sufficient statistic, making MDL and sufficiency operationally equivalent in that setting.
